---
title: "Correlation & Regression"
---

<span class="section-badge">Basics</span>

Regression allows you to model the relationship between a continuous outcome variable (the **dependent variable**, DV) and one or more predictors (the **independent variables**, IVs). Start with correlation to quantify the relationship between two variables, then move to regression when you want to predict an outcome or test the unique contribution of each predictor.

This page covers:

- [Correlation](#correlation) — quantifying a relationship between two continuous variables
- [Simple regression](#simple-regression) — predicting an outcome from one predictor
- [Multiple regression](#multiple-regression) — predicting an outcome from several predictors, controlling for the others

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Regression is ANOVA, just continuous
Quick framing: regression keeps the same explained-variance logic from ANOVA, but swaps categorical for continuous predictors — and JASP's Regression module instead of ANOVA.
:::

---

## Correlation

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — which student traits predict the kind of lecturer they like?
Chamorro-Premuzic et al. (2008) measured five student personality traits (Big Five) and five dimensions of *preferred* lecturer style (knowledgeable, charismatic, accessible, etc.). The natural first move is a full correlation matrix — every trait against every lecturer dimension — to see which pairings hang together and in what direction. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 7).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 2 below](#exercise-2-correlation-matrix-which-student-traits-predict-the-lecturer-they-prefer).
:::

### What is it?

A **correlation coefficient** (*r*) describes the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two continuous variables. Pearson's *r* ranges from −1 (perfect negative) to +1 (perfect positive), with 0 indicating no linear relationship.

| *r* | Conventional label |
|---|---|
| ±.10 | Small |
| ±.30 | Medium |
| ±.50 | Large |

### When to use it

- As a descriptive measure of association between two variables (no implied causality)
- As a preliminary step before regression
- When reporting relationships in your results section

### Assumptions

::: {.assumption-list}
- Both variables are **continuous** (interval/ratio)
- The relationship is **linear** (check with a scatter plot — non-linear relationships yield misleading *r* values)
- Approximately **bivariate normal** — no extreme outliers (outliers can inflate or deflate *r* dramatically)
:::

If assumptions are violated, use Spearman's *ρ* (rho) — a rank-based non-parametric alternative available in JASP.

### How to do it in JASP

Go to **Regression → Correlation**.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Correlation
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#correlation](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#correlation):

- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#main-analysis) — Pearson/Spearman correlation matrix
- [▶ Plots](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#plots) — scatter plots and correlation matrix visualisation
- [▶ Partial Correlation](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#main-analysis-partial) — controlling for a third variable
:::

---

## Simple regression

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — does advertising spend predict album sales?
A music label tracked the advertising budget (€) and first-week album sales for a sample of releases. A simple linear regression of sales on advertising estimates two numbers: the predicted sales of an album that gets *no* advertising (the intercept *b₀*), and how much each extra Euro of advertising buys you in expected sales (the slope *b₁*). The same dataset returns later on this page in the APA reporting example. Source: DSUJ ch. 8 (album sales running example).
:::

### What is it?

Simple (linear) regression models the relationship between **one predictor** (X) and a **continuous outcome** (Y):

$$Y = b_0 + b_1 X + \varepsilon$$

- *b₀*: the intercept — the predicted value of Y when X = 0
- *b₁*: the slope — the expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in X
- *ε*: the residual error

The **R²** statistic tells you the proportion of variance in Y explained by the model. Adjusted R² penalises for the number of predictors and is used for model comparison.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why the intercept (*b₀*) belongs in the model
You might be tempted to drop the intercept when it isn't substantively meaningful (e.g. when X = 0 makes no sense). Don't — omitting it forces the regression line through the origin, which introduces systematic over- and under-estimation. The reported *R²* can then look misleadingly high because it's compared against a degenerate null model that predicts zero rather than the mean.

→ [Worked example with album sales](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/IncludingIntercept.html)
:::

### Assumptions

::: {.assumption-list}
- **Linearity**: the relationship between X and Y is linear — check with a scatter plot
- **Independence of residuals**: each observation is independent — check with the Durbin-Watson statistic
- **Homoscedasticity**: the spread of residuals is constant across fitted values — check the residuals vs. fitted plot
- **Normality of residuals**: residuals should be approximately normally distributed — check the residuals Q-Q plot
- **No influential outliers**: cases with high Cook's distance (> 1 as a rough rule) warrant investigation
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Diagnosing homoscedasticity at a glance
Plot the standardised residuals against the standardised predictions. A "healthy" cloud of points means error is constant across the range of Y. A funnel shape or a tilted line means the model is more accurate for some predicted values than others — a sign of mis-specification.

→ [Visual catalogue: healthy vs. heteroscedastic plots](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/Heteroscedasticity.html)
:::

### How to do it in JASP

Go to **Regression → Linear Regression**.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Simple Regression
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression):

- [▶ Single Predictor](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#main-analysis-single-predictor) — simple regression setup and output
- [▶ Assumption Checks](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#assumption-checks-1) — residual plots, Q-Q plots
- [▶ Cook's Distance](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#cooks-distance) — identifying influential cases
:::

---

## Multiple regression

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — does narcissism predict Facebook status frequency over and above extraversion?
Ong et al. (2011) measured adolescents' status-update frequency, demographics (age, grade, sex), extraversion, and narcissism. The interesting question isn't whether narcissism *correlates* with status posts — extraversion does too, and the two traits overlap. A hierarchical multiple regression enters demographics first (Model 0), extraversion next (Model 1), and narcissism last (Model 2); the ΔR² at each step shows what each block adds *over and above* what's already in. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 8).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 3 below](#exercise-3-hierarchical-multiple-regression-does-narcissism-predict-facebook-status-frequency-above-and-beyond-extraversion).
:::

### What is it?

Multiple regression extends simple regression to **two or more predictors**:

$$Y = b_0 + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots + b_k X_k + \varepsilon$$

Each *bⱼ* is a **partial regression coefficient** — it represents the unique effect of predictor *Xⱼ* on Y, *controlling for all other predictors in the model*. This is a crucial distinction from simple regression or from looking at correlations in isolation.

### Building your model

There are several approaches to entering predictors:

| Method | When to use |
|---|---|
| **Simultaneous (Enter)** | All predictors entered at once. Use when you have theory-driven reasons to include all predictors |
| **Hierarchical** | Predictors entered in blocks based on theoretical priority. Change in R² (ΔR²) for each block tests the incremental contribution |
| **Stepwise** | Automated selection based on statistical criteria — generally discouraged for confirmatory research but can be useful exploratory |

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Hierarchical regression in JASP
How to set up Model 0 → Model 1 → Model 2 in JASP's Regression module, what the ΔR² between consecutive models actually means, and why "controlling for X" and "adding X to the null model" are the same operation.
:::

### Additional assumption: multicollinearity

When predictors are highly correlated with each other, regression coefficients become unstable. Check:
- **Tolerance** (should be > .1) or **VIF** (variance inflation factor, should be < 10)
— both available in JASP under *Collinearity Diagnostics*

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## VIF, tolerance, and why 10 isn't a hard cutoff
What multicollinearity really means (predictors that overlap), how to read VIF (the 10 threshold is a continuum, not a binary pass/fail), and why a scatter plot matrix of your predictors is still worth its weight — VIF only catches *linear* dependence.
:::

### How to do it in JASP

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Multiple Regression
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#regression):

- [▶ Multiple Predictors](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#main-analysis-multiple-predictors) — multiple regression setup and output
- [▶ Hierarchical Regression](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#hierarchical-regression) — entering predictors in blocks, ΔR²
- [▶ Bootstrap Confidence Intervals](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_Regression.html#bootstrap-confidence-interval) — robust inference for the coefficients
:::

---

## Interpreting the regression output

The key tables in JASP output:

**Model Summary**

| Column | What it means |
|---|---|
| **R** | Multiple correlation coefficient |
| **R²** | Proportion of variance in Y explained by all predictors combined |
| **Adjusted R²** | R² corrected for the number of predictors — use this for model comparison |
| **RMSE** | Root mean square error — average prediction error in the original units |

**Coefficients table**

| Column | What it means |
|---|---|
| **b** (Unstandardised) | The slope in original units — "one unit increase in X is associated with a *b* change in Y, holding all other predictors constant" |
| **β** (Standardised) | The slope in standard deviation units — comparable across predictors with different scales |
| **t** / **p** | Whether the slope is significantly different from zero |
| **95% CI** | Confidence interval for the slope |

::: {.callout-tip}
## APA reporting example
Album sales were significantly predicted by advertising budget, *b* = 0.096, 95% CI [0.076, 0.115], *β* = .578, *t*(196) = 9.98, *p* < .001. The model explained 33% of the variance in album sales, *F*(1, 198) = 99.6, *p* < .001, adjusted *R²* = .33.
:::

---

## Dummy coding categorical predictors

Continuous predictors enter regression directly. Categorical predictors must be **dummy coded**: create *k* − 1 binary variables (0/1), where *k* is the number of categories. JASP does this automatically when you define a variable as nominal.

**Example**: a variable with 3 groups (control, therapy A, therapy B) → 2 dummy variables. The reference category (control) becomes the baseline against which the others are compared.

::: {.callout-tip}
## How predictions and group means come out of the dummies
The intercept becomes the mean of the reference group; each dummy's coefficient is the difference from that group. Add a covariate (or another categorical predictor) and the coefficients shift slightly — the dummies no longer recover the group means *exactly*, but the prediction formula still works the same way. With an interaction term added, you do recover each cell mean — at the cost of more parameters.

→ [Part 1: one categorical predictor + optional covariate](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/PredictingWithDummies_1Variable.html) · [Part 2: two predictors and the interaction term](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/PredictingWithDummies_2Variables.html)
:::

---

## Further reading

- **DSUJ Chapter 7** — *Correlation*
- **DSUJ Chapter 8** — *Regression* (simple and multiple, hierarchical, dummy coding)
- JASP blog — [Hierarchical regression](https://jasp-stats.org/2017/10/18/hierarchical-regression-jasp-0-8-3/)

---

## Further viewing — SSR lecture recordings

The 2026 SSR lectures cover correlation and regression across three recordings (captions are available in the SharePoint player):

- [▶ SSR Lecture 7 — Correlation & the Linear Model](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/r_a_voskens_uva_nl/EYmgPRhAgkBEliafLv_ech8BPwxS8gy8aTtX7lOVHJvFbg?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=eV0zUx) — correlation up front; jump to **~23:28** for the linear-model / simple-regression section
- [▶ SSR Lecture 8 — The Linear Model](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/ESL3M4DpFURCuLyCb7ILp24BYREu8vRoKWYd7tN-4v4kWw?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=nqlXIF) — simple linear regression in depth
- [▶ SSR Lecture 22 — Multiple Regression](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/EVZu2_XUBChDgKCE_D74OZQBnFoilT4XQRG5cEDfZcWu3g?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJPbmVEcml2ZUZvckJ1c2luZXNzIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXciLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJNeUZpbGVzTGlua0NvcHkifX0&e=OFlsC3) — multiple predictors, hierarchical regression, multicollinearity

---

## Try it yourself

Each exercise below ends with a collapsible solution. Try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

### Exercise 1 (correlation) — Do GCSE maths grades predict university statistics grades?

The [`grades.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_data/data/grades.jasp) dataset pairs each student's GCSE maths grade with their later university statistics grade. Pick the appropriate correlation coefficient (think about the measurement level of GCSE grade), run it, and report *r* with its 95% CI.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Smart Alex Task 7.4](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/smart_alex#task-7.4)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [alex_07_04.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_alex/alex_07_04.html)
:::

### Exercise 2 (correlation matrix) — Which student traits predict the lecturer they prefer?

Chamorro-Premuzic et al. (2008) measured five student personality traits and five lecturer-preference dimensions. Using [`leni_07.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_07.jasp), produce the full correlation matrix between student traits and preferred-lecturer dimensions, and identify which student trait is most strongly associated with each lecturer dimension.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Why do you like your lecturers?](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#why-do-you-like-your-lecturers)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_07.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_07.html)
:::

### Exercise 3 (hierarchical multiple regression) — Does narcissism predict Facebook status frequency above and beyond extraversion?

Ong et al. (2011) measured adolescents' status-update frequency, demographics (age, grade, sex), extraversion, and narcissism. Using [`leni_08a.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_08a.jasp), fit a hierarchical regression with **three blocks**: demographics (Model 0), + extraversion (Model 1), + narcissism (Model 2). Report ΔR² for each step.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — I want to be loved (on Facebook)](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#i-want-to-be-loved-on-facebook)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_08a.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_08a.html)
:::
